title: React State and Effects
summary: useState, useEffect, dependency arrays, and the mental model for managing side effects in React components.

questions.prompt: What does `useState` actually return?
choices: A mutable variable and a re-render trigger
A frozen snapshot of state for the current render, and a setter that schedules the next render with a new snapshot
A global variable shared across all renders
A ref that persists between renders
questions.explanation: State is a snapshot — it is frozen for the duration of one render. The setter queues a new render with the new value. This is why multiple `setCount(count + 1)` calls in one event handler only increment once — they all read the same frozen snapshot.

questions.prompt: What is `count` after clicking the button?
choices: An error
questions.explanation: All three setCount calls read the same frozen snapshot (count = 0). So all three schedule `setCount(0 + 1)`. Result: 1. To increment three times, use the functional form: `setCount(prev => prev + 1)` which chains on the queued value.

questions.prompt: What three behaviours does the useEffect dependency array control?
choices: How often the component renders, mounts, and unmounts
No array: run every render. Empty []: run once on mount. Populated [deps]: run when any dep changes
Whether effects are synchronous, async, or lazy
Which props trigger a state update
questions.explanation: These are the three forms: `useEffect(fn)` = after every render; `useEffect(fn, [])` = once on mount; `useEffect(fn, [a, b])` = when a or b changes. The dependency array is how you tell React which external values your effect cares about.

questions.prompt: When does a useEffect cleanup function run?
choices: Only when the component unmounts
Before the next effect runs (when deps change) AND when the component unmounts
Before every render
Immediately after the effect runs
questions.explanation: Cleanup runs in two cases: 1) when the component unmounts, 2) before the effect re-runs due to a dependency change. This ensures old subscriptions, timers, and connections are torn down before new ones are established.

questions.prompt: What happens with this interval counter?
choices: Counts up correctly: 1, 2, 3...
Stays at 1 forever — count in the closure is stale (always 0)
Throws an error after the first increment
Counts up but resets on every re-render
questions.explanation: The empty dep array means the effect runs once. The closure captures `count = 0` at mount time and never refreshes. Every tick sets count to 0+1=1. Fix: `setCount(prev => prev + 1)` — functional update reads the latest value without needing count in the closure.

questions.prompt: Why should you NOT use `useEffect(async () => { ... }, [])` directly?
choices: async functions are not supported inside components
An async function returns a Promise. useEffect interprets the return value as a cleanup function — a Promise is not a valid cleanup function
It causes infinite loops
Async effects don't have access to state
questions.explanation: useEffect expects the callback to return either nothing or a cleanup function (a synchronous function). An async callback returns a Promise. React will warn and ignore it as cleanup — meaning no cleanup runs at all. Pattern: define an async function inside the effect body and call it.

questions.prompt: What is lazy initialisation of useState and when should you use it?
choices: Passing a function to useState instead of a value — the function runs only once on mount, not on every render
Calling useState only when needed
Delaying state creation until first render
Using null as initial state
questions.explanation: `useState(() => expensiveCalc())` — the function is called once. `useState(expensiveCalc())` — the function is called on EVERY render (even though the result is thrown away after mount). Use lazy init whenever the initial value is expensive to compute.

questions.prompt: Which of these correctly mutates state for an object in React?
choices: state.name = 'Alice'
setState(prev => ({ ...prev, name: 'Alice' }))
setState({ ...state, name: 'Alice' })
Object.assign(state, { name: 'Alice' })
questions.explanation: React requires a new object reference to detect state changes. Options B and C both create new objects. B uses functional update (safer — reads latest state). C directly uses the captured `state` variable (snapshot — fine in most cases but can be stale in async code). A and D mutate the existing object — React will NOT detect the change.

questions.prompt: In React 18, calling setState multiple times in the same event handler results in:
choices: Multiple re-renders — one per setState call
One re-render — all setState calls are batched automatically (including in async events)
An error — multiple setState in one function is not allowed
One re-render only in class components
questions.explanation: React 18 introduced automatic batching — all setState calls within any event handler, setTimeout, fetch callback, or native event are batched into a single re-render. In React 17 and earlier, batching only applied to React event handlers.

questions.prompt: A component has `useEffect(() => { fetchUser(id); }, [id])`. When does the effect fire?
choices: Every render
On mount, and whenever `id` changes
Only when the component unmounts
Only once, on mount
questions.explanation: With `[id]` as the dep array, React runs the effect after the initial mount AND after any render where `id` has changed (compared using Object.is). If id stays the same between renders, the effect is skipped.

questions.prompt: What problem does the `active` boolean flag solve in a useEffect fetch pattern?
choices: Prevents the fetch from running twice in StrictMode
Prevents a stale response from updating state after the component has unmounted or the id dep has changed
Makes fetches run in parallel
Replaces the need for a cleanup function
questions.explanation: If `id` changes while the first fetch is in-flight, the cleanup sets `active = false`. When the stale response arrives, `if (active) setUser(data)` is skipped — preventing setting state with old data for a different user. It also prevents the 'setState on unmounted component' warning.

questions.prompt: React StrictMode in development mounts components twice. The purpose is:
choices: Testing server-side rendering
Detecting missing cleanup functions — if your effect breaks on double-mount, cleanup is incomplete
Improving performance by pre-computing renders
Preventing use of deprecated APIs
questions.explanation: StrictMode mounts → unmounts → remounts to simulate what happens when React suspends and resumes components (a pattern used in concurrent features). If your effect has no cleanup, the second mount may set up duplicate subscriptions, WebSockets, or intervals.

questions.prompt: When two sequential setState calls happen in a React 18 `setTimeout`, they:
choices: Are NOT batched — each causes a separate re-render
ARE batched — one re-render (React 18 automatic batching)
The second call is silently ignored
Both calls are deferred to the next microtask
questions.explanation: React 18 automatic batching extends to setTimeout, fetch callbacks, and native event handlers — not just React event handlers. Both setState calls are grouped into one re-render. Use `flushSync` if you need immediate separate renders.

questions.prompt: If `setState` is called with the same value as current state (Object.is equality), React:
choices: Always re-renders
Bails out — skips the re-render entirely
Re-renders but skips DOM updates
Throws an error
questions.explanation: React uses Object.is to compare the new state value with the old. If they're the same (same primitive value or same object reference), React bails out and skips re-rendering the component and its children. This is an important optimisation.

questions.prompt: What does this component's useEffect do on each render?
choices: Fetches once on mount
Fetches after every render — including the render triggered by setUser, creating an infinite fetch loop
Never fetches because setUser is not in deps
Fetches twice due to StrictMode only
questions.explanation: No dep array means 'run after every render'. The fetch sets user state → triggers re-render → effect runs again → another fetch → infinite loop. Fix: add `[]` to run once, or `[userId]` to run when the relevant id changes.

questions.prompt: Where should you NOT put state that can be derived from other state?
choices: In a parent component
In useState — derived values belong in regular variables or useMemo, not additional state
In useRef
In a context provider
questions.explanation: Derived state creates synchronisation problems. If `fullName = firstName + ' ' + lastName`, don't store fullName in state — compute it during render: `const fullName = firstName + ' ' + lastName`. Storing redundant state leads to bugs where the derived state gets out of sync.

questions.prompt: A useEffect with `[user.id]` as its dependency: if `user` object reference changes but `user.id` stays the same, does the effect re-run?
choices: Yes — the user reference changed
No — React compares by the dep values listed, and user.id is the same (Object.is)
It depends on the React version
Yes — any parent re-render triggers all effects
questions.explanation: React compares each individual dependency with Object.is. `user.id` is read at render time and compared to its previous value. If `user.id` (a primitive string/number) is the same, the effect does NOT re-run — even if the `user` object reference changed.

questions.prompt: Which of these are valid cleanup operations to return from useEffect?
choices: () => clearInterval(id)
() => subscription.unsubscribe()
() => controller.abort()
async () => await fetch('/cleanup')
questions.explanation: Cleanup must be a synchronous function (or nothing). Options A, B, and C are all valid synchronous cleanup operations. D is an async function — invalid as cleanup (the returned Promise is not handled). If you need async cleanup, fire-and-forget inside the cleanup function.

questions.prompt: The `react-hooks/exhaustive-deps` ESLint rule warns when:
choices: You use too many hooks
A variable used inside useEffect is not listed in the dependency array — a common source of stale closure bugs
You call hooks conditionally
You use useState without a setter
questions.explanation: exhaustive-deps ensures every value from the component scope used inside useEffect is listed in deps. Missing deps = stale closures. The rule sometimes suggests adding functions to deps — the solution is usually to move the function inside the effect or wrap it in useCallback.

questions.prompt: What is the correct way to fetch data inside useEffect when the fetch needs to be cancellable?
choices: Use a try-catch around the fetch
Use AbortController — create it before the fetch, pass signal to fetch options, return `() => controller.abort()` as cleanup
Store a cancelled boolean in state
Use useRef to hold the fetch promise and cancel it
questions.explanation: AbortController is the standard Web API for cancelling fetch requests. Pass `controller.signal` to the fetch options. When cleanup runs (dep change or unmount), `controller.abort()` causes the fetch to reject with an AbortError — which you can ignore in the catch handler.

flashcards.front: State is a snapshot, not a variable
flashcards.back: Each render gets its own frozen snapshot of state. Multiple setState calls in one event all read the same snapshot. Use functional updates `setState(prev => ...)` to chain on queued values.
No array = run every render. [] = run once on mount. [a,b] = run when a or b changes. Always include every variable used inside the effect (exhaustive-deps rule).
Effect captures variables at creation time. If deps are missing, the effect sees stale values. Fix with functional setState `prev => ...` or by adding the variable to deps.
1) Before next effect run (dep changed). 2) On component unmount. Always return a cleanup for subscriptions, timers, WebSockets, and in-flight fetches.
`useState(() => expensive())` — function called once. `useState(expensive())` — called on every render. Use function form for expensive computations.
Never mutate state directly. React compares by reference. Always create a new object: `setState(prev => ({ ...prev, key: value }))`.
Don't `useEffect(async () => ...)`. Define async fn inside and call it: `useEffect(() => { const run = async () => {...}; run(); }, [dep])`.
All setState calls in event handlers, setTimeout, fetch callbacks, and native events are batched into one re-render. Use `flushSync` to opt out.

project.brief: Design a `useUserData(userId)` custom hook that: 1) fetches user data from `/api/users/:id`, 2) shows a loading state while the request is in flight, 3) handles errors gracefully with a `error` field, 4) cancels the in-flight request if userId changes before the response arrives, 5) avoids updating state after the component unmounts. Write out the full hook with useState and useEffect only (no libraries). Then explain: what happens in StrictMode during development? What does your cleanup do to prevent the double-mount issue?

checklist.text: Hook returns { user, loading, error } — all three states covered
Uses AbortController and passes signal to fetch — cleanup calls controller.abort()
Dep array is [userId] — re-fetches when userId changes, not on every render
Sets loading=true before fetch and loading=false in finally block
Explains StrictMode: abort fires on first unmount; second mount starts fresh fetch — abort gracefully handles the AbortError in catch

hints: Start with three state variables: user, loading (true), error (null).
AbortController pattern: create before fetch, return () => controller.abort() as cleanup, check for AbortError in catch.
In catch block: `if (err.name !== 'AbortError') setError(err.message)` — ignore intentional aborts.
StrictMode: the first cleanup aborts the first fetch. The second mount starts a new fetch that completes normally.
